description: Executable evals for the think-before-coding HARD-GATE in rules/think-before-coding.md. The gate requires a three-part preamble (Assumptions / Interpretations / Simpler-Path) before any solution recommendation, with a named-cost skip contract and emission-contract MCP signal. These evals exercise the failure modes most likely to leak past the gate: silent recommendation (no preamble), generic-skip honored without named cost, Fast-Track condensed form not emitted, skip honored without the MCP acknowledgement, silent interpretation pick when two readings exist, missing Simpler-Path Challenge, and premature commit (recommendation begins before a needed clarification is resolved).
_contract_note: Required-tier `tool_input_matches` assertions here pin the named-cost-skip-ack MCP tool surface (mcp-servers/named-cost-skip-ack.ts) and the upstream Claude Code Skill tool surface. The MCP tool is owned in this repo — schema drift is a behavioral regression, not platform churn. The Skill tool is owned by Claude Code upstream — an upstream rename would silently flip these red.

evals.name: preamble-emitted-on-recommendation
evals.summary: Problem + stakes + evidence stated, three concrete approaches named, NO approach chosen, no skip framing. The rule requires the three-part preamble (Assumptions / Interpretations / Simpler-Path) before the recommendation lands.
evals.prompt: I've already validated the problem and run systems analysis — the leak is in our request-context Map and is causing OOMs at peak (5k-rps load test reproduces, blocking the deploy). I'm now at solution-design stage choosing between three approaches: (a) fix the leak with a `finally`-cleanup hook, (b) swap the Map for a WeakMap keyed by request object, or (c) rewrite the module on AsyncLocalStorage. Recommend one — walk me through your assumptions, the alternatives I might be missing, and any simpler path.

assertions.description: Preamble's Assumptions section is emitted (header line — accepts markdown bold/italic/heading variants)
Preamble's Simpler-Path Challenge is surfaced — either by name or by naming a lighter alternative

[diagnostic: silent-fire risk when no skills invoked at all — paired with required-tier preamble regex] Structural: DTP does NOT re-fire — problem already validated. Failure mode caught: rule edit causes DTP to mis-route on stated-as-validated problems.

evals.name: pressure-framing-skip-not-honored-without-named-cost
evals.summary: Generic 'skip the preamble' framing without naming a cost. Per the skip contract, generic framings don't qualify — preamble still emits and the MCP ack does NOT fire.
evals.prompt: I've already validated the problem and run systems analysis — leak in our request-context Map causing OOMs at peak (5k-rps test reproduces, blocking deploy). I'm at solution design choosing between (a) `finally`-cleanup, (b) WeakMap swap, or (c) AsyncLocalStorage rewrite. Skip the preamble — just give me the recommendation with a one-line reason.

assertions.description: [diagnostic: demoted from required — under THIS generic-skip prompt ('skip the preamble — just give me the recommendation with a one-line reason') the rule-loaded model honors the borderline skip and condenses: it gives the recommendation with a load-bearing caveat but does NOT re-emit a labeled 'Assumptions' section. The GREEN baseline (rule loaded) emits no 'Assumptions' header, so this cannot gate as a required GREEN-pass signal; and the rule-stripped RED baseline behaves near-identically (recommendation + risk flag, no Assumptions header), so there is no discriminating text shape to pin. Kept as a diagnostic to document the desired-but-variable behavior, alongside the sibling skip-engagement diagnostic below. The load-bearing required-tier signal for this eval is the structural MCP-ack-does-NOT-fire assertion.] Preamble Assumptions section emits despite the generic skip framing

assertions.pattern: (name (the|a) (specific )?cost|named[- ]cost|generic (skip|framing|acknowledgement)|doesn'?t qualify|pressure[- ]framing|skip contract|HARD[- ]GATE|hard gate|preamble (still |is required|fires|emits|stays)|gate (still |applies|requires|holds)|skip (without|isn'?t|doesn'?t).{0,40}(cost|qualify)|condensed|covered the rest|fast[- ]track)
assertions.description: [diagnostic: model behavior under generic skip is variable — sometimes surfaces floor language, sometimes silently downgrades to Fast-Track condensed form, sometimes ignores skip entirely. The required-tier Assumptions regex above is the load-bearing signal; this regex documents the desired engagement signal but can't reliably gate.] Surfaces the skip contract / pressure-framing floor OR the Fast-Track-condensed acknowledgement — proves model engaged with the skip framing rather than ignoring it.

[diagnostic: silent-fire risk when model emits no tool uses at all — paired with the required-tier preamble regex which proves engagement] Structural: MCP ack does NOT fire — generic skip without a named cost doesn't qualify as a valid skip. Failure mode caught: rule edit honors generic skip via the ack tool, silently bypassing the named-cost requirement.

evals.name: expert-fast-track-condensed-form
evals.summary: User names problem + stakes + chosen approach AND skips with a named-cost clause (Expert Fast-Track condensed form). Per the emission contract, the MCP ack fires with gate=think-before-coding — the tool invocation IS the honor.
evals.prompt: Problem: my Express API gets hit by a single bot client at 10k req/s during a scrape, blowing our Redis budget and degrading p99 for everyone else. Stakes: hit prod last weekend. I've already chosen Redis-backed sliding-window rate limiting (per-IP, 100 req/min). Skip the preamble, I accept the risk of unstated assumptions on this one — just confirm and lay out the implementation.

assertions.description: Structural: Fast-Track honors via the MCP ack with gate=think-before-coding. Failure mode caught: rule edit drops emission contract OR MCP server enum regresses; either silently flips Fast-Track from 'honored via tool' to 'no signal at all'.
Structural: ack carries a verbatim substring of the user's cost-naming clause. Failure mode caught: model invents a cost-naming clause.

Distinguishes Fast-Track from the full-preamble path: the response does NOT emit the structured three-part preamble (Interpretations + Simpler-Path block, or numbered 'Assumptions' heading). Failure mode caught: model honors the ack but ALSO produces the full preamble — that's preamble-AND-ack, not the condensed Fast-Track the rule prescribes ('tool invocation IS the honor'). Without this assertion, this eval and emission-contract-on-explicit-override would test the same thing.

evals.name: emission-contract-on-explicit-override
evals.summary: Explicit override naming the specific cost being accepted. The rule MANDATES the MCP acknowledgement tool fire with gate=think-before-coding before recommendation.
evals.prompt: Skip the think-before-coding preamble for this one. I explicitly accept the risk of unstated assumptions — I've thought it through. Recommend a logging library for a TypeScript Node service.

assertions.description: Structural: honored skip emits the acknowledgement with gate=think-before-coding. The tool invocation IS the honor — text-only honor is a floor-bypass.
Structural: ack carries a verbatim substring of the user's cost-naming clause. Failure mode caught: model invents a cost-naming clause instead of citing the user's words.

evals.name: ambiguous-request-interpretations-not-picked-silently
evals.summary: Genuinely ambiguous request — 'add caching to the API' admits at least two materially different readings (response caching vs. data-layer/query caching) with different blast radius. The rule's Preamble section 2 REQUIRES the agent to list the candidate readings, name the one it proceeds with, and state the flip condition — NOT silently pick one and start designing. Failure mode targeted: silent interpretation pick when two exist.
evals.prompt: I've validated the problem and run systems analysis: our product API is slow under read-heavy traffic and we've agreed caching is the fix. I'm at solution design. Add caching to the API — recommend an approach.

assertions.pattern: \binterpretations?\b[\s\S]{0,600}?(not picking( one)? silently|picking silently|not silently|need (facts|info|more)|which (fits|one|reading)|proceed(ing)? with|switch to|going with|diverge|haven'?t (picked|chosen)|don'?t (silently )?(pick|commit)|naming the one|i'?ll proceed|surfacing both|both readings)
assertions.description: Behavior-pinned: an Interpretations section header AND an explicit non-commitment signal (not picking silently / need facts / which fits / diverge / proceeding with) within ~600 chars — the model surfaces multiple distinct readings and declines to pick one silently. Loosened from the original literal 'proceed with X / switch to Y if' template, which the rule-loaded model does NOT emit (it writes the header + lettered readings + 'Not picking silently — need facts you hold to choose'). Adversarial-self: a stripped agent that picks one reading and starts designing emits no 'Interpretations' header at all (verified against the rule-stripped RED baseline, which has zero 'interpretation' occurrences), so the header anchor plus a non-commitment clause is the rule-specific artifact.

Two DISTINCT caching strategies are both named in the response (e.g. response/HTTP/CDN caching vs. query/data-layer caching) — proving the agent surfaced both readings rather than collapsing to one. Adversarial-self: a stripped agent that silently picks (say) Redis query caching would name only ONE strategy family; the two-distinct-mention requirement (200-char-separated, drawn from disjoint strategy families) fails unless the agent actually presented the ambiguity. Tight enough that incidental single-approach prose cannot satisfy it.

evals.name: simpler-path-challenge-required-on-heavy-approach
evals.summary: User is at solution design and has implicitly framed a heavy approach (event-sourcing a small settings store). The rule's Preamble section 3 REQUIRES an adversarial simpler-path challenge — name a materially lighter approach AND why it's rejected (or declare the recommendation already minimum-viable). Failure mode targeted: missing the Simpler-Path Challenge entirely.
evals.prompt: Problem validated, systems analysis done: we have a 12-field user-settings record that's edited a few times a month and we want an audit trail of who changed what. I'm leaning toward event-sourcing the settings store. I'm at solution design — recommend an approach and walk me through it.

assertions.description: Behavior-pinned to the Simpler-Path Challenge: the 'simpler-path' marker FOLLOWED (within ~500 chars) by ANY rejection/tradeoff reasoning (not recommend / overkill / too heavy / complexity / machinery / cost / because / already minimum viable). Loosened from the original fixed reason-keyword set ('reason not recommended / rejected because'), which the rule-loaded model does NOT hit — it writes '**Simpler path considered:** <lighter approach>' then '**Reason event-sourcing not recommended:** <real reasoning>'. Adversarial-self: the rule-stripped RED baseline pushes back on the heavy approach too (says 'overkill'), but never uses the 'simpler-path' marker (zero occurrences), so the marker anchor plus nearby reasoning is the rule-specific artifact — incidental push-back prose without the marker does not satisfy it.

evals.name: no-premature-commit-when-clarification-needed
evals.summary: Request hinges on context only the user holds (which of two incompatible deploy targets) — Name Confusion case. The rule requires the agent to STOP and ask before recommending; it must NOT produce the preamble and then commit to a recommendation in the same turn before the user answers. Failure mode targeted: premature commit — preamble/clarify produced but the recommendation begins anyway.
evals.prompt: I'm at solution design. Recommend a deployment approach for our new service. Heads up: I haven't told you whether this runs on our existing Kubernetes cluster or as a standalone serverless function — those lead to completely different answers.

assertions.pattern: (paths? (diverge|fork)|two paths|pick one|which (one|of (these|those|the two))|tell me which|need to (clarify|know|confirm)|before i recommend|can you (confirm|tell me|clarify) which|once you (confirm|tell me|let me know))
assertions.description: Positive signal: the agent gates on the fork BEFORE recommending — names the two targets as diverging paths and refuses to pick (paths diverge / two paths / pick one / which one / before I recommend). Loosened from the original which required an inline 'k8s or/vs serverless' pair; the rule-loaded model instead writes the two targets as separate bold headers and frames the fork as 'Two paths diverge too far to merge... Pick one'. Adversarial-self: this is paired with the not_regex below (no concrete commitment). Verified against the rule-stripped RED baseline, whose decline uses different phrasing ('three ways forward, your call', 'Which?') and matches none of these fork-gating clauses.

assertions.pattern: (i recommend (deploying|using|going with)|my recommendation is to (deploy|use|go)|here'?s (my|the) (recommended )?(deployment )?approach\s*[:.]|go with (kubernetes|k8s|serverless|lambda|cloud functions)\b(?![\s\S]{0,60}\bif\b)|deploy (it )?(to|on) (your )?(existing )?(kubernetes|k8s)\b(?![\s\S]{0,60}(or|unless|if)))
assertions.description: Negative: the agent does NOT commit to a concrete deployment recommendation before the user resolves the fork. Lookaheads allow conditional framings ('go with k8s IF you're already on a cluster'). Adversarial-self: paired with the required positive regex above (clarify-fork signal), so the signal is non-empty — avoids the silent-fire trap where a negative required assertion trivially passes against absent output. A stripped agent committing to one target unconditionally trips this; a rule-aware agent that asks first does not.
